text:
hello all!

im a software engineer by trade but very interested in forward looking programming languages as a hobby.

im not sure exactly how i stumbled across this slack, but i have been lurking the programminglanguages subreddit for awhile and believe i saw it linked there.

i was drawn to the topic of programming language design and theory by my experimentation with haskell over the years, and the intense curiosity i found in the haskell community for pushing the language to its limits. since then i have consistently been very drawn to various functional languages, particularly purely functional stuff. the recent thing that has been interesting me is algebraic effects, particularly in the ability system provided by <http://unisonweb.org|unisonweb.org>. i have been doing some tentative explorations into a lambda calculus based language myself, drawing inspiration from haskell, unison, reasonml/ocaml, and to a lesser extent, idris, purescript, etc. the syntax will probably be somewhat divergent from the traditions of these languages, as i am interested in seeing how the lambda calculus can be made more approachable for people used to c, java, javascript, python, etc

What do you think about the syntax of Reason, given that the main goal is to provide a syntax for O'Caml that is more friendly to Javascript programmers?

text: have what i can only categorize in my head as a "random" question, so feel free to point me to a better channel for this if necessary: (question about Abstract Binding Trees in thread, trading indirection for channel spam)

text:
i have been interested in the use of ABT's to deal with variables and abstraction in a lambda calculus based language. <https://semantic-domain.blogspot.com/2015/03/abstract-binding-trees.html> provides a helpful introduction for the unfamiliar.

one of the questions that i am interested in but havent yet been able to drum up any answers for is: when does the ABT make itself valuable, and are there contexts where it would be beneficial to transform an ABT based tree to a simple AST or vice versa? the initial context this came up to me is in a hypothetical language that, like haskell, has a distinct frontend syntax and a "core" syntax, an explicitly typed, desugared calculus that provides a stable base for the various language extensions to build on. it is my understanding that generally, the frontend haskell syntax is renamed to avoid capture, and thus most operations on the core syntax don't require this information to be tracked (although i do note that GHC does have the code to perform capture avoiding substitution on core expression trees). my question is, if a language uses an ABT, would it be possible to "save work" by implementing the frontend syntax in a traditional AST style, desugar to an ABT, and substitute away? or vice versa, if the frontend is implemented with an ABT, all typechecking and such is performed then, desugaring to an AST?

in a way, i guess part of my question is contingent on a related question: during what set of compiler operations is capture avoiding substitution relevant? typechecking and evaluation are obvious ones, but what about implementing optimizations on a reduced core expression language?

is there a "sweet spot" for where an ABT adds value in the compiler pipeline, or is it something that's better to go "all in" on?

attachments.title: Abstract Binding Trees

```foo(a b c)```
given that C, Java, Javascript and Python all use `foo(a,b,c)`.

text: Rendering text at a distance helps.  We had some readability success in having labels appear at a distance yet composed after the rest of the scene.  As a result, holding an object in front of a label would cause the object to be disappear, but it did make labels (lines of code in this case) easy to read.

text:
If you use traditional function call syntax, `f(x,y,z)`, then some of the consequences are:
• `f((x,y,z))` passes a single argument, which is a tuple, just like Python. `f(x,y,z)` is something quite different: it is a function call with 3 arguments.
• `f([x,y,z])` passes a single argument, which is a list, just like Python. `f[x,y,z]` is something quite different, probably array indexing syntax.
Does Pynchon support partial application?

elements.text: If you use traditional function call syntax,
f(x,y,z)
, then some of the consequences are:
f((x,y,z))
passes a single argument, which is a tuple, just like Python.
f(x,y,z)
is something quite different: it is a function call with 3 arguments.
f([x,y,z])
passes a single argument, which is a list, just like Python.
f[x,y,z]
is something quite different, probably array indexing syntax.
Does Pynchon support partial application?

text: yes, my plan was to essentially translate `f(x y)` to `f(x)(y)`

elements.text: yes, my plan was to essentially translate
f(x y)
to
f(x)(y)

text: Org-Mode is indeed a nice notebook tools, if notebook tools is what you want. It's even better in permitting Knuth-style literate programming as well. Unfortunatly, if you combine all those features the result is brittle and hard to understand for a reader. And for the author, the big limitation of Emacs is synchronous execution of code snippets. If you run a one-minute computation, Emacs freezes for one minute.

Jonathan Blow talking about his past, of course about games, but also about programming today, open source, C++, Rust, the future of programming, and many other issues people here will be interested in (and/or annoyed by his perspective ;-):
<https://oxide.computer/blog/on-the-metal-9-jonathan-blow/|https://oxide.computer/blog/on-the-metal-9-jonathan-blow/>

attachments.title: Oxide Computer Company: On the Metal: Jonathan Blow
attachments.text: On this episode of On the Metal, we interview legendary game designer and programmer Jonathan Blow. Join along as Jonathan takes us from collision detection on the Atari 800, through porting Doom to SGI's famous set-top box, starting a game company at the worst possible time, his adventures with the (in)famous Cell processor, making several hit games -- and his more recent experiences with a new programming language of his own creation.

text:
Python: f(a, -b, c+d, g(x))
Haskell: f a (-b) (c+d) (g x)
Lisp: (f a (- b) (+ c d) (g x))
Pynchon: f(a (-b) (c+d) (g(x)))

So Pynchon has a novel syntax for function calls. It seems to be quite verbose, requiring more parentheses than Lisp.

elements.text:
Python: f(a, -b, c+d, g(x))
Haskell: f a (-b) (c+d) (g x)
Lisp: (f a (- b) (+ c d) (g x))
Pynchon: f(a (-b) (c+d) (g(x)))

So Pynchon has a novel syntax for function calls. It seems to be quite verbose, requiring more parentheses than Lisp.

text:
> you could omit one pair of parens around g(x) unless i’m mistaken?
I don't think you can do that. Consider
```f((g(x+y)))```
If you omit one pair of parens, then you get
```f(g(x+y))```
which I think is a call to `f` with two arguments, `g` and `(x+y)`, unless white space is significant in the parsing.

text: i intend to require whitespace to separate arguments in function calls, and generally i think i want `identifer()` to always represent a function call. perhaps this could be addressed in the underlying source representation where indentation-based blocks are converted to braced syntax, a la haskell: `f(g(x y) z)` could be syntax sugar for `f(g(x;y);z)` . the use of `;` is obviously a bit odd, but since this is just meant to give us a whitespace insensitive representation, i dont expect it to get used very much, vs the braced syntax which i intend to make a bit more "front and center" for fans of c style syntax.
